State management is one of those problems that every React developer eventually has to solve — and solve well. As applications grow, so does the complexity of keeping server data, UI state, and client-only state in sync. The TanStack ecosystem (created by Tanner Linsley) offers a modern, type-safe answer to this problem, and it pairs beautifully with Next.js.
In this post, we’ll walk through:

• Why TanStack is a great fit for state management

• The difference between server state and client state

• Setting up TanStack Query in a Next.js (App Router) project

• Setting up TanStack Store for lightweight client-side state

• A full working example combining both
1. Why TanStack for State Management?
Most “state management” conversations conflate two very different problems:
Type of State Description Best Tool Server State Data that lives on a server and is fetched/cached (API data, DB records) TanStack Query Client State Data that lives only in the browser (UI toggles, form values, filters) TanStack Store / useState
TanStack Query (formerly React Query) handles caching, background refetching, deduplication, and…
